reasoning:
The question asks whether an independence referendum for Alberta will be officially scheduled before July 1, 2025 (within the next 54 days from the knowledge cutoff of May 7, 2025). 

The UCP government, led by Premier Danielle Smith, recently tabled Bill 54 to lower the signature threshold for citizen-initiated referendums from 20% of eligible voters (~600,000) to 10% of voters in the last general election (~177,000) and extend the collection period to 120 days. Premier Smith explicitly announced on May 5, 2025, that her government does not support separation and will *not* initiate an independence referendum themselves. Instead, they will place the question on a 2026 ballot only if a citizen-led petition successfully gathers the required signatures under the new legislation.

Given this stated policy, the government will not proactively schedule the referendum. The only path to the referendum being officially scheduled is through the citizen initiative process. Within the 54 days before July 1, 2025, it is practically impossible for the entire required sequence to occur: Bill 54 must receive Royal Assent, proponents must have their petition approved, they must collect 177,000 signatures, and Elections Alberta must verify all signatures before the government officially schedules the vote. Because this administrative and signature-gathering process will take several months at a minimum, the referendum cannot be officially scheduled before July 1, 2025. I assign a baseline 5% chance to a "black swan" political crisis that somehow causes the premier to change her stance and unilaterally schedule a snap referendum.
